content:
# DIAGENETIC, PETROPHYSICAL, GEOCHEMICAL AND MICRO-PALEONTOLOGICAL ANALYSIS OF PLEISTOCENE-HOLOCENE CORES ALONG THE KING FAHD CAUSEWAY BETWEEN SAUDI ARABIA AND BAHRAIN

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the diagenetic, petrophysical, and micropaleontological characteristics of Pleistocene-Holocene carbonate and siliciclastic sediments along the King Fahd Causeway. It identifies two main lithofacies—dolomitic and non-dolomitic—and links their depositional environments to significant sea-level fluctuations in the Arabian Gulf.

---

## Research Problem

Limited research exists on the subtidal Pleistocene-Holocene sediments of the Arabian Homocline/Arabian Shallow Shelf. Specifically, there is a need to understand how these sedimentary sequences respond to Quaternary sea-level fluctuations in the region between Saudi Arabia and Bahrain.

## Objectives

- Identify and describe the composition and texture of the Pleistocene-Holestone sediments and lithofacies along the Causeway trajectory.
- Improve understanding of the nature and distribution of the sediments.
- Identify and understand diagenetic processes in the carbonate sequences as well as the depositional and diagenetic history.
- Link depositional and diagenetic processes with Pleistocene-Holocene sea level changes in this part of the Arabian Gulf.
- Determine the depositional sequence along a carbonate ramp in response to a rise in sea level.
- Describe development of sedimentation patterns on a carbonate ramp in response to sea-level fluctuations and/or tectonic processes.

## Methods

The study involved core description (macroscopic properties, minerals, and structures), thin-section petrography for mineralogy and diagenesis, XRD for bulk mineral composition, XRF for elemental analysis, SEM-EDX for microtexture and mineral morphology, QEMSCAN for high-resolution mineral mapping, stable isotope analysis (δ18O and δ13C) of carbonate samples, and micropaleontology to identify benthic foraminiferal assemblages. Petrophysical measurements included porosity and permeability using an automated permeameter/porosimeter.

## Data and Materials

Analysis was performed on 21 cores from the Saudi Arabian side of the Causeway; specifically, three core sections (B 14, B 24, and B 44.3) were selected for detailed study. A total of 62 thin sections were prepared for petrography, and 36 samples were used for micropaleontological analysis.

## Key Findings

- Two distinct mineralogical units were identified: a lower dolomitic unit (approx. 40m thick) and an upper non-dolomitic unit (3-9m thick). ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- The lower dolomite lithofacies includes oolitic grainstone, skeletal packstone, wackestone-packstone, pelletal skeletal packstone-grainstone, and wackestone-mudstone. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- The upper non-dolomitic unit consists of interbedded quartz sandstone, calcitic skeletal grainstone, quartz sand, aragonitic skeletal grainstone, and aragonitic oolitic sand/grainstone. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- Thirteen lithofacies were identified based on lithology, color, texture, grain size, visible porosity, and sedimentary structure. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- Dolomitic interval shows several shallowing upward depositional cycles consisting of skeletal packstone, wackestone-mudstone, pelletal packstone, and green mudstone. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- The study identifies a transition from a shallow marine environment to a restricted lagoonal environment in the dolomitic units. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 19]])
- Aragonitic components were found to dissolve and calcite cement precipitate during periods of lower sea level or increased aridity. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 20]])
- The non-dolomitic units' lithostratigraphy and diagenesis match the sea level changes profile. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 20]])
- XRD analysis of core BR1 samples showed specific mineral composition percentages for various lithofacies (e.g., aragonitic oolitic sand, quartz sandstone). ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 88]])
- XRF data was converted to determine weight percentages of MgCO3, CaCO3, and SiO2 across different core samples. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 96]])
- Two main lithological units were identified: a non-dolomitic unit (aragonitic oolitic sand/grainstone, aragonitic skeletal grainstone, quartz sand, calcitic skeletal grainstone, and quartz sandstone) and a dolomitic unit (dolomitic oolitic grainstone, dolomitic skeletal packstone, dolomitic skeletal wackestone, dolomitic pellet skeletal packstone-grainstone, and green mudstone). ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 137]])
- The non-dolomitic unit shows evidence of early diagenetic changes including cementation (aragonitic and calcitic) and micritization. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 92]])
- In the dolomitic units, significant diagenetic changes include dissolution-precipitation phases where aragonite was leached or recrystallized into dolomite. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 100]])
- XRD analysis of non-dolomitic samples showed mineral compositions of approximately 50% aragonite, 30% calcite, and 20% quartz/feldspar for oolitic sand; 60% aragonite, 30% calcite, and 5-10% quartz/feldspar for skeletal grainstone. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 87]])
- XRF analysis of core BR1 showed high calcium (Ca) content (>70%) and significant magnesium (Mg), with higher iron (Fe) in the green mudstone indicating diagenetic effects. ([[2022-khan|Khan, 2022, p. 116]])

## Limitations

The study notes that the sedimentary scenario is highly speculative as no age dates were available to validate the specific sequence in some sections (Page 126).

## Recommendations and Future Work

The study suggests that understanding the translation from sediment properties to acoustic properties is necessary for future seismic profiles to position foundations for new pillars of the Causeway extension.
